questions.q: What is the main purpose of a journal in accounting?
opts: (A) To record transactions in the ledger
(B) To post transactions to the ledger
(C) To record and classify business transactions
(D) To prepare financial statements
questions.explanation: A journal is used to record and classify business transactions, making it easier to post them to the ledger and prepare financial statements.

questions.q: Which of the following is an example of a personal account?
opts: (A) Cash
(B) Building
(C) Capital
(D) Drawings
questions.explanation: Drawings is a personal account, representing the owner's withdrawals from the business.

questions.q: What is the process of transferring journal entries to the ledger called?
opts: (A) Posting
(B) Journalizing
(C) Ledgerizing
(D) Accounting
questions.explanation: Posting is the process of transferring journal entries to the ledger, where they become part of the permanent accounting records.

questions.q: Which of the following accounts is a real account?
opts: (A) Sales
(B) Purchase
(C) Building
(D) Capital
questions.explanation: Building is a real account, representing a tangible asset of the business.

questions.q: What is the purpose of a ledger account?
opts: (A) To record transactions in the journal
(B) To post transactions to the ledger
(C) To provide a permanent record of business transactions
(D) To prepare financial statements
questions.explanation: A ledger account provides a permanent record of business transactions, making it easier to prepare financial statements.

questions.q: A company purchases office supplies worth Rs. 5,000. What is the journal entry for this transaction?
opts: (A) Debit Office Supplies Rs. 5,000, Credit Cash Rs. 5,000
(B) Debit Office Supplies Rs. 5,000, Credit Office Expenses Rs. 5,000
(C) Debit Office Supplies Rs. 5,000, Credit Cash Rs. 10,000
(D) Debit Office Expenses Rs. 5,000, Credit Cash Rs. 5,000
questions.explanation: The journal entry for purchasing office supplies is a debit to Office Supplies and a credit to Office Expenses, since it's a prepaid expense.

questions.q: A company sells goods worth Rs. 20,000 on credit to a customer. What is the journal entry for this transaction?
opts: (A) Debit Sales Rs. 20,000, Credit Cash Rs. 20,000
(B) Debit Sales Rs. 20,000, Credit Accounts Receivable Rs. 20,000
(C) Debit Cash Rs. 20,000, Credit Sales Rs. 20,000
(D) Debit Accounts Receivable Rs. 20,000, Credit Sales Rs. 20,000
questions.explanation: The journal entry for selling goods on credit is a debit to Accounts Receivable and a credit to Sales, since the customer still owes the business the amount.

questions.q: What is the rule for debiting and crediting a revenue account?
opts: (A) Debit revenue accounts and credit asset accounts
(B) Credit revenue accounts and debit expense accounts
(C) Debit asset accounts and credit revenue accounts
(D) Debit expense accounts and credit revenue accounts
questions.explanation: Revenue accounts are credited and expense accounts are debited, since revenue increases owners' equity and expenses decrease it.

questions.q: A company purchases a piece of land worth Rs. 1,50,000. What is the journal entry for this transaction?
opts: (A) Debit Land Rs. 1,50,000, Credit Cash Rs. 1,50,000
(B) Debit Cash Rs. 1,50,000, Credit Land Rs. 1,50,000
(C) Debit Land Rs. 1,50,000, Credit Office Expenses Rs. 1,50,000
(D) Debit Office Expenses Rs. 1,50,000, Credit Land Rs. 1,50,000
questions.explanation: The journal entry for purchasing a piece of land is a debit to Land and a credit to Cash, since the business gives up cash to acquire the asset.

questions.q: What is the difference between a nominal account and a real account?
opts: (A) Nominal accounts are asset accounts and real accounts are liability accounts
(B) Nominal accounts are expense accounts and real accounts are asset accounts
(C) Nominal accounts are expense accounts and real accounts are asset accounts
(D) Nominal accounts are asset accounts and real accounts are liability accounts
questions.explanation: Nominal accounts are expense accounts or revenue accounts, while real accounts are asset accounts, liability accounts, or equity accounts.

questions.q: A company incurs an expense of Rs. 10,000 for salaries. What is the journal entry for this transaction?
opts: (A) Debit Salaries Expense Rs. 10,000, Credit Cash Rs. 10,000
(B) Debit Cash Rs. 10,000, Credit Salaries Expense Rs. 10,000
(C) Debit Salaries Expense Rs. 10,000, Credit Office Expenses Rs. 10,000
(D) Debit Office Expenses Rs. 10,000, Credit Salaries Expense Rs. 10,000
questions.explanation: The journal entry for incurring an expense is a debit to Salaries Expense and a credit to Cash, since the business gives up cash to incur the expense.

questions.q: What is the purpose of the trial balance?
opts: (A) To prove the equality of debit and credit balances in the ledger
(B) To prepare financial statements
(C) To record transactions in the journal
(D) To post transactions to the ledger
questions.explanation: The trial balance is a tool to prove the equality of debit and credit balances in the ledger, ensuring that the accounting records are accurate.

questions.q: A company purchases office equipment worth Rs. 20,000. The seller offers a 5% discount for cash payment. What is the journal entry for this transaction?
opts: (A) Debit Office Equipment Rs. 20,000, Credit Cash Rs. 20,000
(B) Debit Office Equipment Rs. 20,000, Credit Cash Rs. 19,000
(C) Debit Office Equipment Rs. 19,000, Credit Cash Rs. 19,000
(D) Debit Office Equipment Rs. 19,000, Credit Cash Rs. 20,000
questions.explanation: The journal entry for purchasing office equipment with a discount is a debit to Office Equipment and a credit to Cash for the net amount paid, which is 95% of the original price.

questions.q: A company sells goods worth Rs. 30,000 on credit to a customer. The terms of the sale are 2/10, n/30. What is the journal entry for this transaction?
opts: (A) Debit Sales Rs. 30,000, Credit Accounts Receivable Rs. 30,000
(B) Debit Sales Rs. 30,000, Credit Discounts on Sales Rs. 3,000, Credit Accounts Receivable Rs. 27,000
(C) Debit Accounts Receivable Rs. 30,000, Credit Sales Rs. 30,000
(D) Debit Accounts Receivable Rs. 30,000, Credit Sales Rs. 27,000
questions.explanation: The journal entry for selling goods on credit with a discount is a debit to Sales, a credit to Accounts Receivable, and a credit to Discounts on Sales for the discount amount, which is 2% of the sale price.

questions.q: What is the rule for debiting and crediting a liability account?
opts: (A) Debit liability accounts and credit asset accounts
(B) Credit liability accounts and debit asset accounts
(C) Debit asset accounts and credit liability accounts
(D) Debit expense accounts and credit liability accounts
questions.explanation: Liability accounts are debited and asset accounts are credited, since liabilities increase and assets decrease when a business incurs a liability.

questions.q: A company purchases a piece of land worth Rs. 1,50,000. The seller offers a 2% commission to the realtor. What is the journal entry for this transaction?
opts: (A) Debit Land Rs. 1,50,000, Credit Cash Rs. 1,50,000
(B) Debit Land Rs. 1,50,000, Credit Cash Rs. 1,48,500
(C) Debit Land Rs. 1,48,500, Credit Cash Rs. 1,48,500
(D) Debit Land Rs. 1,48,500, Credit Cash Rs. 1,50,000
questions.explanation: The journal entry for purchasing land with a realtor's commission is a debit to Land and a credit to Cash for the net amount paid, which is 98% of the original price.

questions.q: What is the purpose of the ledger account?
opts: (A) To provide a permanent record of business transactions
(B) To record transactions in the journal
(C) To post transactions to the ledger
(D) To prepare financial statements
questions.explanation: The ledger account provides a permanent record of business transactions, making it easier to prepare financial statements and analyze business performance.

questions.q: A company sells goods worth Rs. 40,000 on credit to a customer. The terms of the sale are 3/10, 2/30. What is the journal entry for this transaction?
opts: (A) Debit Sales Rs. 40,000, Credit Accounts Receivable Rs. 40,000
(B) Debit Sales Rs. 40,000, Credit Discounts on Sales Rs. 1,200, Credit Accounts Receivable Rs. 38,800
(C) Debit Accounts Receivable Rs. 40,000, Credit Sales Rs. 40,000
(D) Debit Accounts Receivable Rs. 40,000, Credit Sales Rs. 38,800
questions.explanation: The journal entry for selling goods on credit with multiple discounts is a debit to Sales, a credit to Accounts Receivable, and a credit to Discounts on Sales for the discount amount, which is 3% of the sale price.
